briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for every node in the tree.

detaileddescription.para: The macro fn must take one parameter, which will be a node identifier. The macro is expanded once for each node in the tree. The order that nodes are visited in is not specified.

briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for every node in the tree with multiple arguments.

detaileddescription.para: The macro fn takes multiple arguments. The first should be the node identifier for the node. The remaining are passed-in by the caller.
The macro is expanded once for each node in the tree. The order that nodes are visited in is not specified.

parameterdescription.para: variable number of arguments to pass to fn

briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for every status okay node in the tree.

detaileddescription.para: The macro fn must take one parameter, which will be a node identifier. The macro is expanded once for each node in the tree with status okay (as usual, a missing status property is treated as status okay). The order that nodes are visited in is not specified.

briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for every status okay node in the tree with multiple arguments.

detaileddescription.para: The macro fn takes multiple arguments. The first should be the node identifier for the node. The remaining are passed-in by the caller.
The macro is expanded once for each node in the tree with status okay (as usual, a missing status property is treated as status okay). The order that nodes are visited in is not specified.

parameterdescription.para: variable number of arguments to pass to fn

briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for each ancestor of node_id.

detaileddescription.para: The macro fn must take one parameter, which will be the identifier of a child node of node_id to enable traversal of all ancestor nodes.
The ancestor will be iterated over in the same order as they appear in the final devicetree.
Example devicetree fragment:
Example usage:
This expands to:

briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for each child of node_id.

detaileddescription.para: The macro fn must take one parameter, which will be the node identifier of a child node of node_id.
The children will be iterated over in the same order as they appear in the final devicetree.
Example devicetree fragment:
Example usage:
This expands to:

briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for each child of node_id with a separator.

detaileddescription.para: The macro fn must take one parameter, which will be the node identifier of a child node of node_id.
Example devicetree fragment:
Example usage:
This expands to:

parameterdescription.para: Separator (e.g. comma or semicolon). Must be in parentheses; this is required to enable providing a comma as separator.

briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for each child of node_id with multiple arguments.

detaileddescription.para: The macro fn takes multiple arguments. The first should be the node identifier for the child node. The remaining are passed-in by the caller.
The children will be iterated over in the same order as they appear in the final devicetree.

parameterdescription.para: variable number of arguments to pass to fn

briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for each child of node_id with separator and multiple arguments.

detaileddescription.para: The macro fn takes multiple arguments. The first should be the node identifier for the child node. The remaining are passed-in by the caller.

parameterdescription.para: Separator (e.g. comma or semicolon). Must be in parentheses; this is required to enable providing a comma as separator.
variable number of arguments to pass to fn

briefdescription.para: Call fn on the child nodes with status okay

detaileddescription.para: The macro fn should take one argument, which is the node identifier for the child node.
As usual, both a missing status and an ok status are treated as okay.
The children will be iterated over in the same order as they appear in the final devicetree.

briefdescription.para: Call fn on the child nodes with status okay with separator.

detaileddescription.para: The macro fn should take one argument, which is the node identifier for the child node.
As usual, both a missing status and an ok status are treated as okay.

parameterdescription.para: Separator (e.g. comma or semicolon). Must be in parentheses; this is required to enable providing a comma as separator.

briefdescription.para: Call fn on the child nodes with status okay with multiple arguments.

detaileddescription.para: The macro fn takes multiple arguments. The first should be the node identifier for the child node. The remaining are passed-in by the caller.
As usual, both a missing status and an ok status are treated as okay.
The children will be iterated over in the same order as they appear in the final devicetree.

parameterdescription.para: variable number of arguments to pass to fn

briefdescription.para: Call fn on the child nodes with status okay with separator and multiple arguments.

detaileddescription.para: The macro fn takes multiple arguments. The first should be the node identifier for the child node. The remaining are passed-in by the caller.
As usual, both a missing status and an ok status are treated as okay.

parameterdescription.para: Separator (e.g. comma or semicolon). Must be in parentheses; this is required to enable providing a comma as separator.
variable number of arguments to pass to fn

briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for each element in the value of property prop.

detaileddescription.para: The macro fn must take three parameters: fn(node_id, prop, idx). node_id and prop are the same as what is passed to DT_FOREACH_PROP_ELEM(), and idx is the current index into the array. The idx values are integer literals starting from 0.
The prop argument must refer to a property that can be passed to DT_PROP_LEN().
Example devicetree fragment:
Example usage:
This expands to:

In general, this macro expands to: fn(node_id, prop, 0) fn(node_id, prop, 1) [...] fn(node_id, prop, n-1) where n is the number of elements in prop, as it would be returned by DT_PROP_LEN(node_id, prop).

briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for each element in the value of property prop with separator.

detaileddescription.para: Example devicetree fragment:
Example usage:
This expands as a first step to:
The prop parameter has the same restrictions as the same parameter given to DT_FOREACH_PROP_ELEM().

parameterdescription.para: Separator (e.g. comma or semicolon). Must be in parentheses; this is required to enable providing a comma as separator.

briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for each element in the value of property prop with multiple arguments.

detaileddescription.para: The macro fn must take multiple parameters: fn(node_id, prop, idx, ...). node_id and prop are the same as what is passed to DT_FOREACH_PROP_ELEM(), and idx is the current index into the array. The idx values are integer literals starting from 0. The remaining arguments are passed-in by the caller.
The prop parameter has the same restrictions as the same parameter given to DT_FOREACH_PROP_ELEM().

parameterdescription.para: variable number of arguments to pass to fn

briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for each element in the value of property prop with multiple arguments and a separator.

detaileddescription.para: The prop parameter has the same restrictions as the same parameter given to DT_FOREACH_PROP_ELEM().

parameterdescription.para: Separator (e.g. comma or semicolon). Must be in parentheses; this is required to enable providing a comma as separator.
variable number of arguments to pass to fn

briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for each status okay node of a compatible.

detaileddescription.para: This macro expands to: fn(node_id_1) fn(node_id_2) ... fn(node_id_n) where each node_id_<i> is a node identifier for some node with compatible compat and status okay. Whitespace is added between expansions as shown above.
Example devicetree fragment:
Example usage:
This expands to one of the following: "/a" "/c" "/c" "/a" "One of the following" is because no guarantees are made about the order that node identifiers are passed to fn in the expansion.
(The /c string literal is present because a missing status property is always treated as if the status were set to okay.)
Note also that fn is responsible for adding commas, semicolons, or other terminators as needed.

parameterdescription.para: Macro to call for each enabled node. Must accept a node_id as its only parameter.

briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for each status okay node of a compatible with multiple arguments.

detaileddescription.para: This is like DT_FOREACH_STATUS_OKAY() except you can also pass additional arguments to fn.
Example devicetree fragment:
Example usage:
This expands to one of the following:
i.e. it sets x to 7. As with DT_FOREACH_STATUS_OKAY(), there are no guarantees about the order nodes appear in the expansion.

parameterdescription.para: Macro to call for each enabled node. Must accept a node_id as its only parameter.
Additional arguments to pass to fn

briefdescription.para: Call fn on all nodes with compatible compat and status okay with multiple arguments.

parameterdescription.para: Macro to call for each enabled node. Must accept a devicetree compatible and instance number.
Additional arguments to pass to fn

briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for each node label of a given node.

detaileddescription.para: The order of the node labels in this macro's expansion matches the order in the final devicetree, with duplicates removed.

Node labels are passed to fn as tokens. Note that devicetree node labels are always valid C tokens (see "6.2 Labels" in Devicetree Specification v0.4 for details). The node labels are passed as tokens to fn as-is, without any lowercasing or conversion of special characters to underscores.
Example devicetree fragment:
Example usage:
This expands to:

parameterdescription.para: node identifier whose node labels to use
macro which will be passed each node label in order

briefdescription.para: Invokes fn for each node label of a given node with multiple arguments.

detaileddescription.para: This is like DT_FOREACH_NODELABEL() except you can also pass additional arguments to fn.
Example devicetree fragment:
Example usage:
This expands to:

parameterdescription.para: node identifier whose node labels to use
macro which will be passed each node label in order
additional arguments to pass to fn

briefdescription.para: IMPORTANT: you can't use the DT for-each macros in their own expansions.

detaileddescription.para: For example, something like this won't work the way you might expect:
In this example, the C preprocessor won't expand the DT_FOREACH_NODE() macro inside of FOO() while it's already expanding DT_FOREACH_NODE() at the top level of the file.
This is true of any macro, not just DT_FOREACH_NODE(). The C language works this way to avoid infinite recursions inside of macro expansions.
If you need to "nest" calls to one of these macros, you can work around this preprocessor limitation by using a different, related macro instead, like this:
Here, we use DT_FOREACH_NODE_VARGS() "inside" BAR() "inside" DT_FOREACH_NODE(). Because of this, the preprocessor will expand both DT_FOREACH_NODE_VARGS() and DT_FOREACH_NODE() as expected.
